lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market manufacturers of other concrete products that repeatedly provide external customers with engineered-to-order production, estimating, project coordination, scheduled delivery, installation support, inspection documentation, or similar accountable outsourced programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure commodity or one-off product sales without a recurring outsourced-service relationship; concrete block, brick, and pipe manufacturing classified elsewhere; ready-mix operations; captive plants; shells; internal units; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Mostly project or purchase-order revenue from contractors, utilities, transportation agencies, developers, and industrial customers, with eligibility limited to repeat programs where engineering, coordination, logistics, installation support, or documentation is a substantive ongoing supplier responsibility.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 327300 occupation mix, not wage-weighted task exposure in 327390. The estimate maps the administrative, sales, estimating, drafting, scheduling, dispatch, quality-documentation, and selected supervisory workload to AI-susceptible tasks while assigning little exposure to physical forming, finishing, maintenance, and material movement; broader-industry and 2016 vintage likely widen error in either direction.
a.rationale: The broader concrete-products workforce was 25.71% production, 8.47% office/administrative support, 6.83% construction, and 5.99% installation/maintenance in 2016. AI exposure is therefore concentrated in the smaller information-handling layer and parts of planning, estimating, quality records, sales support, and dispatch rather than the physical core.
a.change_evidence: A current 327390 payroll extract by SOC and wage, paired with time-and-motion interviews for estimators, schedulers, dispatchers, quality staff, sales support, and plant supervisors, would materially tighten the wage-weighted task share.
caveats: The available detailed occupation mix is for NAICS 327300 and dates to 2016, not the 2026 327390 lens.
NIST manufacturing use cases describe feasible applications, not labor substitution or current non-automation in this industry.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures AI use across all U.S. businesses and NIST summarizes manufacturing adoption and use cases, rather than five-year implementation of this code's exposed tasks. The range discounts broad adoption for small-firm data quality, legacy-system integration, safety, quality assurance, and change-management constraints; the NIST sample may overstate adoption among digitally mature manufacturers.
rho.rationale: AI use across U.S. businesses was 17%-20% in late 2025 through May 2026, and use was lower among the smallest firms, while NIST identifies data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration as barriers. Over five years, practical implementation should extend beyond today's adoption but remain well below full conversion of exposed work.
rho.change_evidence: A 327390-specific survey of current ERP/MES usage, digitized work instructions, machine and quality data availability, AI pilots, implementation budgets, and realized labor-hour savings would materially move the range.
caveats: BTOS is economy-wide, not specific to concrete-product manufacturers or the LMM band.
NIST adoption figures rely on cited manufacturing surveys whose respondent mix may skew toward larger or more technology-oriented firms.
Safety-critical product approvals and customer specifications can slow automation of quality and engineering judgments.

e.rationale: NAICS 327390 is defined as manufacturing concrete products other than block, brick, and pipe. Most firms therefore sell goods rather than a recurring outsourced service. A minority of custom precast and engineered-product firms plausibly qualify because repeat contracts bundle estimating, design coordination, scheduled production/delivery, documentation, and installation support as an accountable program.
e.change_evidence: Classify a stratified sample of the 490 injected LMM-band firms using websites, customer contracts, and revenue detail to measure recurring service-bearing revenue, repeat-customer concentration, and whether coordination or installation support is substantive rather than incidental.
caveats: Eligibility is highly sensitive to contract-level facts that NAICS records do not disclose.
The injected firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE from size-class data and a Paper/Forest Products margin proxy.
Manufacturing plus installation can sometimes be classified to construction instead, biasing eligible firms out of this code.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell reports voluntary broker-supplied sales for broad Glass, Stone and Concrete Manufacturers and the wider small-business market, not a five-year probability for eligible 327390 LMM firms. The range converts observed annual market liquidity and acquisition interest into a cumulative control-transfer probability while discounting repeat listings, very small deals, nonqualifying firms, family transfers, closures, and owner dependence; marketplace coverage likely understates private strategic deals but overrepresents firms actively marketed for sale.
s5.rationale: BizBuySell recorded 22 closed Glass, Stone and Concrete Manufacturer transactions in 2025 and reported an 11% decline in manufacturing transactions overall. The evidence confirms a functioning transfer market but does not establish a denominator, while current market commentary stresses a shortage of acquisition-ready firms and that some owners wind down or transfer internally.
s5.change_evidence: Obtain a five-year transaction census from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and regional brokers, matched to 327390 firms and the EBITDA band, with seller age, transfer type, closure outcomes, and duplicate deals resolved.
caveats: The marketplace category combines multiple manufacturing codes and mostly smaller transactions.
Voluntary broker reporting omits many private strategic and family transfers and provides no eligible-firm denominator.
Heavy plant, environmental diligence, bonding, and customer approvals may reduce transfer completion.

q.rationale: Implemented savings can initially be retained inside quoted project and product prices, particularly when delivery reliability, engineering coordination, certifications, or custom molds differentiate the offer. Retention erodes at rebid and renewal as contractors and public buyers compare quotes, competitors adopt similar tools, and customers press for lower prices; cost escalation and unfavorable mix already create visible margin pressure in precast operations.
q.change_evidence: Review three years of bid files, quote-to-win data, price-escalation clauses, customer concentration, renewal/rebid cadence, gross-margin bridges, and competitor quote spreads for eligible firms; interview procurement managers on expected sharing of supplier productivity gains.
caveats: No source directly separates AI-enabled savings from price, mix, materials, or volume effects.
Retention varies sharply between bespoke engineered products and standardized competitive items.
Public procurement and large general contractors may force faster pass-through than relationship-based private work.

d5.bridge: Census construction spending and public-company precast results measure nominal construction activity and selected company revenue/backlog, not five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand for eligible 327390 service-bearing output. The range strips out an approximate inflation effect qualitatively and balances 2025 total construction weakness against public infrastructure growth and strong water/sewage categories; project timing and regional exposure can create substantially wider firm-level outcomes.
d5.rationale: Nominal U.S. construction value declined 1.4% in 2025, with private construction down 2.9% but public construction up 3.6%; sewage/waste and water-supply categories rose 13.9% and 7.1%. L.B. Foster's precast sales grew 19.9% in 2025 while its precast backlog declined 6.7%, supporting a roughly flat-to-modestly-growing real five-year base case with broad cyclicality.
d5.change_evidence: Build a shipment-weighted end-market mix for eligible 327390 firms and apply current Dodge Construction Network, FMI, PCA, state DOT, water-utility capital-plan, and customer backlog forecasts in real terms through 2031.
caveats: Construction-spending data are nominal and broader than this product code; the primitive requires constant-price demand.
Public-company growth and backlog may reflect share gains, acquisitions, geography, or mix rather than industry demand.
Interest rates, federal grants, state budgets, and local construction cycles can move demand materially.

o.rationale: The qualifying work remains tied to producing, certifying, coordinating, and delivering physical concrete components. Software can streamline the workflow but cannot supply the product or assume quality, schedule, safety, warranty, and logistics accountability. Limited operator displacement could come from customer vertical integration, standardized direct procurement, consolidation into larger suppliers, or substitution to other materials.
o.change_evidence: Interview contractors, utilities, DOT procurement teams, and engineers about planned self-performance, modular procurement, material substitution, and supplier consolidation; examine five years of make-versus-buy decisions and specification changes.
caveats: The estimate concerns operator requirement, not whether employment inside the operator changes.
Material substitution and vertical integration are end-market specific and could be larger in standardized products.
Eligible service-bearing firms are already a small subset of the code.

narrative.executive_view: This is principally a physical-product industry, so the frozen recurring-service lens admits only a small subset of custom and programmatic suppliers. Within that subset, AI can improve the information layer around a durable physical operation, but it does not remove the plant, logistics, quality, or accountable-operator core.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term opportunities are quote preparation, takeoff and specification review, order entry, scheduling, dispatch, purchasing, document control, customer service, predictive maintenance, and computer-vision-assisted inspection. Production, finishing, maintenance, loading, and delivery remain predominantly physical and constrain total wage exposure.
narrative.value_capture: Quoted and engineered work can retain savings between bid cycles, especially where certification, reliability, molds, and delivery complexity create differentiation. Competitive rebids, public procurement, customer concentration, and diffusion of similar tools should pass a meaningful share of gains to customers over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: The injected LMM population is sizable, but eligibility is the binding filter because product-only manufacturers do not meet the outsourced-service lens. A live market exists for glass, stone, and concrete manufacturers, yet transaction data are sparse and acquisition-ready firms face financing, plant, environmental, and owner-dependence diligence.
narrative.demand_durability: Demand is anchored in physical construction and infrastructure. Recent data show private weakness alongside public growth and stronger water and sewage spending, implying a stable central case but meaningful cyclical and end-market dispersion rather than smooth growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the fraction of firms whose bundled services are substantive enough to qualify, the stale broader-industry occupation mix, scarce denominator-based transfer data, and the conversion from nominal construction spending to real demand. Product liability, specifications, legacy systems, data scarcity, local construction cycles, materials inflation, and customer concentration can all reduce implementation or retention.
narrative.principal_driver: Durable need for accountable production and delivery of physical engineered concrete products, with AI improving the surrounding information workflow.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most establishments sell products rather than a recurring outsourced service, sharply limiting eligibility under the frozen lens.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Other Concrete Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Official industry scope: establishments primarily manufacture concrete products other than block, brick, and pipe.

sources.title: May 2016 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 327300
sources.supports: Broader-industry occupation structure: production 25.71%, office/administrative support 8.47%, construction 6.83%, and installation/maintenance 5.99% of employment, with detailed administrative, dispatch, estimating, and production roles.

sources.title: Large Firms With At Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: BTOS showed overall business AI use at 17%-20% from December 2025 to May 2026, expected six-month use at 20%-23%, and lower use among the smallest firms.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing — Text Only
sources.supports: Manufacturing AI use cases in predictive maintenance, quality, forecasting, scheduling, computer vision, document management, and supply-chain optimization; stated barriers include data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration.

sources.title: L.B. Foster Company 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Precast Concrete Products sales rose 19.9% in 2025; higher volume improved gross profit but higher manufacturing costs and mix reduced infrastructure gross margin, while precast backlog fell 6.7%.

sources.title: Northwest Pipe Company 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Precast demand and pricing depend on local construction, infrastructure spending, specifications, competing materials, input costs, compliance, and regional concentration; the filing also documents acquisition activity and operator obligations.

sources.title: BizBuySell 2025 Fourth Quarter Insight Report
sources.supports: Reported 22 closed Glass, Stone and Concrete Manufacturer transactions in 2025; manufacturing transactions fell 11%, and the broader market showed active buyers but limited acquisition-ready supply.

sources.title: Monthly Construction Spending, December 2025
sources.supports: 2025 nominal construction value fell 1.4%; private fell 2.9%, public rose 3.6%, sewage and waste disposal rose 13.9%, and water supply rose 7.1%.
